# JA‚ÜíEN Lyric Translation: Linguistic Difference Rules

Use this file together with `reference/common.md`. Common rules (format protection, workflow, Red Lines, special line handling) are in `common.md`.

**Line length limit (JA‚ÜíEN specific):** Target **6‚Äď16 syllables** per English line; never exceed **18 syllables**. Split at original timeline breakpoints if needed.

---

## 1. Pro-Drop ‚Üí Mandatory Subject Restoration

Japanese regularly omits subjects (pro-drop). English requires explicit subjects in almost all cases. Restore the correct subject based on context.

| Situation | Restoration Strategy | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Song has a clear single narrator | Restore `I` | `ś∂ą„Āą„Ā¶„ĀĄ„ĀŹ` ‚Üí `I disappear` / `fading away` |
| Second-person address | Restore `you` | `Ť¶č„Ā§„āĀ„Ā¶„ĀĄ„Āü` ‚Üí `you were watching` |
| Narrator addressing themselves | `I` or `we` by context | `šŅ°„Āė„Ā¶„Āü` ‚Üí `I believed` |
| Third-person absent referent | `they` / `she` / `he` | `„Ā©„Āď„Āč„ĀłŤ°Ć„Ā£„Ā¶„Āó„Āĺ„Ā£„Āü` ‚Üí `she has gone somewhere` |
| Duet ‚ÄĒ different speakers | Build speaker map first; assign `I`/`you` per block | ‚ÄĒ |
| Impersonal/universal statement | Omit subject or use `we` / `it` | `śôā„ĀĮťĀé„Āé„Ā¶„ĀĄ„ĀŹ` ‚Üí `time keeps passing` |

## 2. Word Order Inversion: SOV ‚Üí SVO

Japanese sentences end with the verb; English places the verb after the subject.

| Japanese Structure | English Target | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `S + O + V` | `S + V + O` | `ŚÉē„ĀģŚŅÉ„āíŚ•™„Ā£„Āü` ‚Üí `stole my heart` |
| Long pre-noun modifier | Relative clause | `ŤľĚ„ĀĄ„Ā¶„ĀüŚźõ„ĀģÁě≥` ‚Üí `your eyes that used to shine` |
| `A„Āó„Ā¶ B„Āó„Ā¶ C„Āô„āč` te-form chain | Coordinated clauses | ‚Üí see Section 4 |
| Sentence-final conditional | Move `if`-clause to front | `Śźõ„ĀĆŚ•Ĺ„Āć„Ā™„āČ` ‚Üí `if I love you` |

## 3. Sentence-Final Particles ‚Üí English Tone

Japanese sentence-final particles carry emotional information that must be restored through English word choice and punctuation.

| Japanese Particle | Meaning/Nuance | English Rendering |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `„Ā≠` | Seeking agreement / softening | "right?" / "you know?" / soft statement ending |
| `„āą` | Assertion / emphasis | `!` / "I tell you" / strong declarative |
| `„Ā™` | Self-talk / sigh | `‚Ä¶` / quiet statement / absorbed into tone |
| `„Āč„Ā™` | Self-questioning / uncertainty | "I wonder" / "maybe" / "perhaps" |
| `„Āģ` | Explanation / softening | Absorbed into sentence: "because‚Ä¶" / "the thing is‚Ä¶" |
| `„Āė„āÉ„Ā™„ĀĄÔľü` | Rhetorical confirmation | "isn't it?" / "right?" |
| `„Ā£„Ā¶` | Quotation / topic shift | "saying" / dash to introduce quote |
| `„Āē` | Casual self-confidence | Absorbed into register; informal phrasing |

## 4. Te-Form Chains ‚Üí English Coordination

Japanese uses te-form („Äú„Ā¶) to chain multiple actions or states. English has several options.

| Chain Type | English Strategy | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sequential actions | "and" / "then" | `Áęč„Ā°šłä„ĀĆ„Ā£„Ā¶ś≠©„ĀŹ` ‚Üí `stand up and walk` |
| Simultaneous actions | "while" / "-ing" participle | `ś≥£„Āć„Ā™„ĀĆ„āČÁ¨Ď„Ā£„Ā¶` ‚Üí `laughing while crying` |
| Causal chain | "so" / "and then" | `Śā∑„Ā§„ĀĄ„Ā¶ś≥£„ĀĄ„Āü` ‚Üí `I got hurt and cried` |
| Long chain split across lines | Split at natural breath; each line self-contained | ‚ÄĒ |

> **Critical rule**: Do NOT translate the entire te-form chain in the first line. Follow the original timeline breaks; each line must be independently readable.

## 5. Verb Tense and Aspect

| Japanese Form | English Equivalent | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| „Äú„Āü (past) | Simple past | `ŤĶį„Ā£„Āü` ‚Üí `ran` |
| „Äú„Ā¶„ĀĄ„āč (ongoing state) | Present progressive or present simple | `ś∂ą„Āą„Ā¶„ĀĄ„ĀŹ` ‚Üí `disappearing` / `fading` |
| „Äú„Ā¶„Āó„Āĺ„Ā£„Āü (regrettable completion) | Perfect + emotional weight | `ŚŅė„āĆ„Ā¶„Āó„Āĺ„Ā£„Āü` ‚Üí `I've forgotten you` / `lost you` |
| „Äú„Ā¶„ĀĄ„Āü (past ongoing) | Past progressive | `šŅ°„Āė„Ā¶„Āü` ‚Üí `I was believing` / `I believed` |
| „Äú„Ā†„āć„ĀÜ / „Äú„Āč„Ā™ (conjecture) | "probably" / "maybe" / "I wonder" | `Ś§ßšłąŚ§ę„Ā†„āć„ĀÜ„Āč` ‚Üí `I wonder if I'll be okay` |
| „Äú„Āü„ĀĄ (desire) | "want to" | `šľö„ĀĄ„Āü„ĀĄ` ‚Üí `I want to see you` |

## 6. Katakana Loanwords

| Category | Strategy | Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Direct English equivalent | Restore the original English word | `„ÉÜ„É¨„ā≠„É£„āĻ„āŅ„Éľ` ‚Üí `Telecaster` |
| Brand / product name | Keep English name | `„É©„āł„ā™` ‚Üí `radio` |
| Japanese-distorted form | Find the closest English equivalent | `„É≠„Éě„É≥„ÉĀ„ÉÉ„āĮ` ‚Üí `romantic` |
| Abstract concept loanword | Use natural English equivalent | `„āĘ„ā§„Éá„É≥„ÉÜ„ā£„ÉÜ„ā£` ‚Üí `identity` |
| Pure rhythm/filler (`„ÉČ„É™„Éľ„É†`, `„É©„ÉĖ`) | Keep English or absorb into meaning | context-dependent |

## 7. Onomatopoeia and Mimetic Words ‚Üí English Sensation Verbs

Japanese has an extremely rich system of onomatopoeia (śď¨ťü≥Ť™ě) and mimetics (śď¨śÖčŤ™ě). Never leave Japanese syllables in the English translation.

| Japanese | Core Sensation | English Equivalent |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `„ÉČ„ā≠„ÉČ„ā≠` | Racing heartbeat | "heart pounding" / "flutter in my chest" |
| `„ā≠„É©„ā≠„É©` | Sparkling, glittering | "glittering" / "sparkling" |
| `„āĹ„ÉĮ„āĹ„ÉĮ` | Restless, anxious | "restless" / "on edge" |
| `„Āľ„āď„āĄ„āä` | Hazy, absent-minded | "in a daze" / "blankly" |
| `„ĀĶ„āŹ„ĀĶ„āŹ` | Floating, airy | "floating" / "like I'm drifting" |
| `„Āć„āá„āć„Āć„āá„āć` | Looking around restlessly | "looking all around" / "glancing about" |
| `„Āź„āč„Āź„āč` | Spinning, going in circles | "spinning" / "swirling" |

## 8. Japanese Register ‚Üí English Register

| Japanese Register | English Strategy |
|:---:|:---:|
| `„Āß„Āô/„Āĺ„Āô` (polite) | Standard conversational; "I will", "please" |
| ŚŹčťĀĒŚŹ£Ť™Ņ (casual, plain form) | Contractions, informal phrasing; "I'm", "gonna" |
| śĖáŤ™ěŤ™Ņ (literary/classical) | Elevated diction; "thee", "doth", archaic phrasing only if consistent with song genre |
| „É≠„ÉÉ„āĮ/„É©„ÉÉ„Éó (rock/rap) | Direct, punchy; colloquial; some profanity if appropriate |

## 9. High-Context Compression ‚Üí English Explicitness

Japanese lyrics often leave subjects, objects, and causal links unstated (high-context). English is a low-context language and may need these made explicit.

- **Implied object**: `ś∂ô„ĀĆŚáļ„āč` ‚Üí "tears fall from my eyes" (not just "tears fall")
- **Implied causation**: `ŚŅÉ„ĀĆÁóõ„ĀĄ` after context about farewell ‚Üí "my heart aches because you're gone"
- **Warning**: Only make explicit what the surrounding lines already imply. Do NOT add new information.

## 10. Common Error Types (JA‚ÜíEN)

**Error 1: Keeping SOV word order in English.**
- Source: `ŚÉē„ĀģŚ£į„ĀĆŚźõ„ĀęŚĪä„ĀĎ„Āį„ĀĄ„ĀĄ„Ā™`
- Wrong: `My voice to you if it reaches, I hope`
- Correct: `I hope my voice reaches you`

**Error 2: Dropping sentence-final particle nuance.**
- Source: `šŅ°„Āė„Ā¶„āč„āą` (strong assertion)
- Wrong: `I believe` (flat)
- Correct: `I do believe` / `I believe in you!`

**Error 3: Translating onomatopoeia as Japanese syllables.**
- Source: `ŤÉł„ĀĆ„ÉČ„ā≠„ÉČ„ā≠„Āô„āč`
- Wrong: `my chest goes doki doki`
- Correct: `my heart is pounding`

**Error 4: Not restoring pro-dropped subject.**
- Source: `ś∂ą„Āą„Ā¶„Āó„Āĺ„ĀĄ„Āü„ĀĄ`
- Wrong: `wants to disappear` (no subject)
- Correct: `I want to disappear`

**Error 5: Over-translating a te-form chain in the first line.**
- Source: `[00:01]ś≥£„ĀĄ„Ā¶` / `[00:02]Á¨Ď„Ā£„Ā¶` / `[00:03]„Āĺ„Āüś≥£„ĀĄ„Āü`
- Wrong: `[00:01]I cried and laughed and cried again`
- Correct: `[00:01]I cried` / `[00:02]then laughed` / `[00:03]and cried again`

## 11. Final Checklist (7 Points)

- [ ] **Subject restored**: Every English line has an explicit subject (unless clearly imperative).
- [ ] **Word order**: SVO order throughout; no SOV artifacts.
- [ ] **Particles**: Sentence-final particle nuances reflected in punctuation and word choice.
- [ ] **Onomatopoeia**: All Japanese śď¨ťü≥Ť™ě/śď¨śÖčŤ™ě rendered as English sensation words; no Japanese syllables remain.
- [ ] **Register**: English register matches the Japanese speech level and song genre.
- [ ] **Line length**: No line exceeds 18 syllables.
- [ ] **No plot additions**: No characters, backstory, or emotions introduced beyond what the source contains.
